title: West African Jollof Rice

ingredients.name: long-grain parboiled rice or basmati, rinsed

subs.name: Long-grain sona masoori or basmati; avoid short-grain varieties that turn too sticky in the pot

ingredients.name: capsicum (red or green bell pepper), roughly chopped

subs.name: Roasted canned red peppers for a sweeter base; or use an extra tomato if capsicum is unavailable

ingredients.name: onion, 2 for blending plus 1 sliced for frying

subs.name: Dried oregano or a pinch of dried basil; thyme is not a common Indian pantry herb so either works

ingredients.name: vegetable stock or water
visual_checks: Vegetable stock deepens the flavour; plain water works
ingredients.notes: Vegetable stock deepens the flavour; plain water works

subs.name: Plain water plus a Maggi or Knorr vegetable stock cube for the same depth of flavour

steps.text: Blend the fresh tomatoes, capsicum and two of the roughly chopped onions together into a smooth puree. Set aside — this tomato-pepper puree is the flavour base of jollof.

Heat oil in a large heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high flame. Add the remaining sliced onion and fry for 5 minutes until beginning to brown at the edges. Add garlic and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the tomato paste and stir-fry for 3 minutes until it darkens from bright red to a deeper brick colour — this removes the raw tinned flavour and adds depth. Do not skip this step.

Pour in the blended tomato-pepper puree. Add chilli powder, cumin, coriander, thyme and bay leaves. Bring to a vigorous boil and cook uncovered on medium-high for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has reduced by almost half and oil is beginning to float on the surface.

Season the reduced sauce generously with salt. Add the rinsed rice and stir to coat every grain. Pour in the stock — it should cover the rice by about 2 cm. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to the absolute minimum and cover tightly with foil and then the pot lid to trap all steam.

Cook on the lowest possible flame for 25–30 minutes until the rice is cooked through and all liquid is absorbed. For authentic smoky jollof flavour, remove the lid for the final 3 minutes to let a lightly charred bottom crust form. Fluff gently and serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: capsicum → Roasted canned red peppers for a sweeter base; or use an extra tomato if capsicum is unavailable; thyme → Dried oregano or a pinch of dried basil; thyme is not a common Indian pantry herb so either works; stock → Plain water plus a Maggi or Knorr vegetable stock cube for the same depth of flavour; rice → Long-grain sona masoori or basmati; avoid short-grain varieties that turn too sticky in the pot
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Basmati rice works very well and is what most Indian cooks will have. The technique is similar to tomato rice (tamatar chawal) but the tomato-pepper base must be reduced for 15 minutes first — this step is essential and should not be skipped. Use a blender (mixie) for the base. Cook in a heavy handi or thick-bottomed pot.
